retrieved.title: u:ai - The AI tool of the University of Vienna
retrieved.text:
To access u:ai, you need your u:account. Access is via [univie.academic-ai.at](https://univie.academic-ai.at)

You have a monthly credit of €20.00 available for queries. The cost of a specific query depends on the length of the question and the length of the answer (file uploads cost more than text queries, shorter texts less than long texts). You can view and monitor your credit balance directly in u:ai. Once the amount has been used up, you will not be able to use u:ai again until the following month.

Since the monthly credit is limited, u:ai should be used specifically for study and university-related questions. The credit is automatically recharged on the first day of each month. Unused credit cannot be carried over to the following month.

**Knowledge Cut-Off:**

U:ai is currently based on information that was available until October 2024. The system does not know anything that happened after that. To incorporate up-to-date information, you must activate the [Internet search](#c1268815).

retrieved.title: Basic framework for AI use
retrieved.text:
Although AI can provide support, it is important to remember that it should not be used to replace the work that is expected of you.

This recommendation can be put into practice by configuring an AI tool in a way that that the support it provides is aligned with the respective learning outcomes. For this purpose, an [AI tutor](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/ai-tutor-for-academic-writing/) can be developed that offers support while at the same time helping to safeguard independent work.

retrieved.title: Maintaining independence: AI-generated or AI-supported?
retrieved.text:
When you use AI in your studies, what is decisive is the extent of your own contribution. A helpful point of reference is the [distinction between AI-generated content and AI-supported work](https://www.europarl.europa.eu/doceo/document/TA-9-2020-0277_DE.html). This distinction can help you assess:

- what qualifies as your own work,
- what may be problematic,
- and how you should render the use of AI transparent.

The practice of directly sourcing passages or entire texts from an AI tool without substantial revision constitutes an infringement of academic integrity. In such instances, the fundamental decisions originate from the AI system, with the individual's contribution being confined to providing the prompt. This practice is in direct contravention of the established principles of academic integrity and may be problematic, particularly if it is not disclosed.

retrieved.title: Basic framework for AI use
retrieved.text:
The use of AI in degree programmes is not generally prohibited, but nor is it permitted without any restrictions. Whether and how you may use AI always depends on the specific context, such as:

- the course type
- the examination or assessment format
- the specific requirements set by the lecturer
- the respective learning objectives
- the [principles of good academic practice](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/good-scientific-practice/#c1269035)

As a general rule, you are responsible for all work that you submit, including any that has been created using AI tools. This implies that:

- You are responsible for the accuracy, quality and traceability of your work.
- Any incorrect statements, distortions or fabricated sources are your responsibility.
- While AI may support you in your work, the substantive contribution and independent thinking must be your own.

retrieved.title: Using AI in Your Studies
retrieved.text: Imagine you are tasked with writing an academic paper. You are aware of the importance of a clear research question, thorough research and a coherent line of argument. Yet instead of doing everything yourself, there is a new option: AI-supported tools can suggest literature, generate outlines, draft opening sentences and improve your writing style. Artificial intelligence has long become part of everyday student life. At the same time, its use in degree programmes raises important questions: What is permitted? Where are the boundaries? And what do students need to be aware of? On these pages, you will find concise and reliable information on the responsible use of AI in degree programmes at the University of Vienna. You will learn under which conditions AI may be used, which specific provisions apply to examinations and what requires particular attention when using AI. The content is based on the current regulations of the University of Vienna and is intended for students of all disciplines. The aim is to support a responsible, transparent and reflective use of AI. [### What Is Artificial Intelligence?](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/what-is-artificial-intelligence/) [### AI in Your Studies: What Is Permitted?](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/ai-in-your-studies-what-is-permitted/) [### Academic work and writing](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/) [### AI and examinations](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/ai-and-exams/) [## u:ai – The University of Vienna's AI tool](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/uai-the-university-of-viennas-ai-tool/) [### Further information](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/further-information/) Generative AI (OpenAI, ChatGPT 5.2) was used on this website and its subpages to adapt content for the target audience. It was

retrieved.title: Basic framework for AI use
retrieved.text: tools may be used therefore depends to a significant extent on the respective learning outcome, which may vary from course to course. Example: - When translating a passage from French in an academic paper on a specific topic, it is important to engage with the concrete content. In this case, the use of an AI translation tool may be unproblematic, provided that the translation is checked for accuracy and the use of AI is disclosed. - In a language degree programme, translation itself is a central learning outcome. Therefore, if a translation task is delegated to an AI tool in this context, the independent performance expected of students would be undermined. If AI performs the work on your behalf, this contravenes the principles of academic integrity. Whether you are permitted to use artificial intelligence in your degree programme therefore primarily depends on whether the required learning outcomes and your independent contribution are preserved.

retrieved.title: u:ai - The AI tool of the University of Vienna
retrieved.text:
u:ai enables the creation of so-called tailored AIs. These are individually configured chatbots that permanently store your instructions.

- Such chatbots can optionally work with their own knowledge base (e.g., guidelines, templates), which is then always given priority. This significantly reduces [hallucinations](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/what-is-artificial-intelligence/#c1268300).
- Once you have built a chatbot, you can also share it with other people.

This means that recurring tasks can be handled more efficiently without having to explain what the AI should do each time.

In certain modules, an Internet search can be activated as an option. This accesses external information via the [Perplexity](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Perplexity.ai) service. This gives you access to up-to-date information.

When Internet search is activated, the entire chat history to date is used as context. This means that personal or confidential data used in this chat leaves the closed system of u:ai. Data protection compliance is then no longer fully guaranteed.

Therefore, never use personal or confidential content in the same chat as the Internet search, but start a new chat for the Internet search.

When using AI in higher education, it is not only functionality that plays a role, but above all the legal and data protection framework in which a tool is operated. U:ai was developed specifically for the university context and differs in key respects from freely accessible, commercial AI offerings.

retrieved.title: u:ai - The AI tool of the University of Vienna
heading_path: Why should I use u:ai? Other AI tools can do so much more!
retrieved.text: u:ai is operated in a secure cloud where all data is processed within the EU. The content you enter is not used for training AI models. This means that any text, documents, or questions you enter into u:ai remain in a protected environment. Bei vielen kommerziellen KI-Tools ist hingegen unklar, wo Daten gespeichert werden, wie lange sie aufbewahrt bleiben und ob sie für Trainingszwecke weiterverwendet werden. Daraus ergibt sich ein erhöhtes Risiko, insbesondere bei der Arbeit mit personenbezogenen, sensiblen oder studienrelevanten Inhalten What does this mean for me? - u:ai is compliant with data protection requirements insofar as your personal data and the information you enter are processed in accordance with the data protection standards of the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR). This does not apply if, within the same chat, you use the web search function or the Mistral model. - When processing the personal data of third parties, the principle of data minimisation applies as a general rule. This means that only the most essential personal data may be collected, processed and stored, in order to achive a specific defined objective. - In order to process the personal data of third parties or research data, you must obtain the consent of the persons concerned. In this context, please refer to the [information on handling research data](https://ctl.univie.ac.at/fileadmin/user_upload/z_ctl_neu/Angebote_fuer_Studierende/Handouts__Leitfaeden_und_Ressourcen/Handouts_und_Leitfaeden/2024_09_16_AI_and_research_data_EN.pdf). - **[Sensitive data](https://miro.com/app/board/uXjVGJDSrGw=/?moveToWidget=3458764658613096304&cot=14)** (this is a category of personal data that is

retrieved.title: AI and Exams
heading_path: Important information for learning with AI
retrieved.text: is increasing dependence on AI tools. If explanations, solution strategies or summaries are regularly delegated to AI, this may impair your ability to think through content independently and to solve problems on your own. Especially at the beginning of your studies, it is therefore important to use AI with restraint and to first develop your own learning strategies.

retrieved.title: AI and Exams
heading_path: Examinations with AI: What does apply here?
retrieved.text: permitted is prohibited. If you are unsure, you should always consult your lecturers in order to clarify expectations and framework conditions at an early stage. - Transparency and documentation: If you are permitted to use AI tools for written assignments, you must [fully disclose](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/citing-and-labelling-ai/#c1269189) such use in accordance with the principles of good academic practice. - Examination formats are changing: You should be prepared for greater emphasis on competences such as reflection and transfer. To ensure the independence of your performance, written assignments may be supplemented by oral components, plausibility checks or grade-relevant discussions. - Personal responsibility: It is imperative that you, the author, accept full responsibility for the accuracy, academic validity and integrity of the results submitted. As language models are not "knowledge machines" and may produce [hallucinations](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/what-is-artificial-intelligence/#c1268979), it is essential that all AI-generated content is critically reviewed. - Consequences of misconduct: The use of unauthorised AI tools constitutes academic misconduct. This results in a non-assessment (entry of an “X”) or the annulment of a grade that has already been awarded. In serious cases, the Rectorate may impose exclusion from studies for up to two semesters.

retrieved.title: What is permitted? And what is not?
retrieved.text:
Whether and how you may use AI in a course or in an examination is determined by the learning outcomes, the fundamental [principles of good academic practice](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/good-scientific-practice/#c1269035) and the requirements set by the respective lecturer. Therefore, always check out

- the information in u:find
- notices on Moodle
- information on specific assignments and examinations

In the event of ambiguity surrounding the use of AI, it is advisable to consult with the lecturer in advance to establish clear regulations. In instances where there is uncertainty, it is prudent to assume that AI is not permitted unless explicit permission has been obtained.

AI may be used, provided that responsibility for the content is retained and the independent nature of the work is preserved. For example, AI may be used to:

- generate ideas or structure topics
- gain an overview of a new topic or prepare for reading
- revise the language of one's own texts
- support reflection or check comprehensibility
- find examples or alternatives which are reviewed and contextualised independently

The use of AI must always be compatible with the requirements set by the lecturer and must not replace your independent contribution.

retrieved.title: u:ai - The AI tool of the University of Vienna
retrieved.text:
u:ai is the University of Vienna's AI platform, providing students with data protection-compliant access to various AI models. The platform was designed specifically for use in a university context and is part of the Austria-wide Academic AI project.

u:ai can support you in your learning, writing, and analysis—you always retain responsibility for the subject matter and control over the content.

#

u:ai is the University of Vienna's official access point to AI models such as ChatGPT in a secure cloud environment. This means:

- All data is processed within the EU (exception: Internet search, Mistral model)
- The content entered will not be used for training AI models.

However, U:ai was not trained with university-specific content. The AI therefore does not have internal knowledge about courses, exams, or internal institute regulations.

retrieved.title: AI and Exams
heading_path: Important information for learning with AI
retrieved.text: The use of AI tools can support the learning process, but it also presents challenges. In order to use AI sensibly and responsibly, it is important to consider a few fundamental aspects. - Due to its [functionality](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/what-is-artificial-intelligence/#c1268970), AI may produce incorrect, incomplete or imprecise information, even if it sounds plausible. Therefore, it is advisable to always compare responses with your own materials. - Subject-specific terminology may be used differently depending on the discipline. When asked to define technical terms, AI generally draws on the meaning most frequently represented in its training data. This may result in definitions that do not correspond to the specific discipline or that lack subject-specific nuances. Only adopt definitions or explanations if they align with the academic requirements of your course. - AI-supported learning tools can heavily structure the learning process, for example through predefined learning paths, automatic revision or recommended content. While this can be helpful, it also entails the risk that your learning process becomes overly externally directed: learning is individual and not every recommendation generated by AI suits your personal learning style. Therefore, consciously set your own priorities. - A common risk factor is increasing dependence on AI tools. If explanations, solution strategies or summaries are regularly delegated to AI, this may impair your ability to think through content independently and to solve problems on your own. Especially at the beginning of your

retrieved.title: Academic work and writing with AI
retrieved.text:
AI can provide valuable support in academic work and writing by generating ideas, structuring texts, revising language and style, organising literature, and clarifying questions of understanding. However, it is important to note that core academic tasks such as developing research questions, constructing arguments, interpreting, and evaluating must be carried out independently.

It is imperative that the use of AI is aligned with the learning outcomes, subjected to critical reflection, and made transparent. Responsibility for content, quality and academic integrity remains with you.

[#### Good scientific practice](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/good-scientific-practice/)

[#### Maintain independence](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/maintaining-independence-ai-supported-or-ai-generated/)

[#### Controlling AI by Prompting correctly](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/controlling-ai-in-a-targeted-manner-the-importance-of-prompting-correctly/)

[#### Citing and labelling AI](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/citing-and-labelling-ai/)

[#### AI in academic writing](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/ai-in-academic-writing/)

[#### Checklist for the responsible use of AI](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/checklist-for-the-responsible-use-of-ai/)

[#### AI and reading academic texts](https://ctl.univie.ac.at/fileadmin/user_upload/z_ctl_neu/Angebote_fuer_Studierende/Handouts__Leitfaeden_und_Ressourcen/Handouts_und_Leitfaeden/2024_09_16_AI_and_reading_academic_texts_EN.pdf)

[#### AI and research data](https://ctl.univie.ac.at/fileadmin/user_upload/z_ctl_neu/Angebote_fuer_Studierende/Handouts__Leitfaeden_und_Ressourcen/Handouts_und_Leitfaeden/2024_09_16_AI_and_research_data_EN.pdf)

retrieved.title: Citing and labelling AI
retrieved.text:
In order to ensure the visibility of your independent contribution, it is essential to present your work transparently and in a manner that allows for clear and accurate tracking of the use of AI. It is crucial to clearly distinguish the parts of the work that originate from you, ensuring that your independent performance is accurately assessed. Failure to disclose the use of AI could be considered a breach of the principles of good academic practice.

The responsibility for determining how the use of AI is to be made transparent lies with the respective lecturer, the study programme director (SPL) or the respective department. In the absence of specific requirements, established best practices may serve as a useful guideline.

A distinction is made between references within the text and entries in the bibliography and, secondly, between content generated entirely by AI and content produced with the assistance of AI.

retrieved.title: Your AI tutor for academic writing
retrieved.text:
AI-supported tools can be customised to align with specific learning outcomes and work requirements. It is quite simple to configure a personalised AI tutor chatbot that supports academic work without compromising independent performance.

- Such an AI tutor can be set up to assume tasks at the linguistic and formal level, for example revising texts, correcting style or organising literature.
- With regard to substantive tasks, such as developing the line of argument, the tutor's role is to provide support and act as a partner in reflection, in the sense of a Socratic dialogue. By posing targeted questions, the tutor can stimulate critical thinking and in-depth engagement with the content, without undermining users' independence or creative contribution.

Configured like this, the AI tutor can offer targeted support without replacing intellectual work.

Recommendation: your AI tutor should first request that you define the learning outcomes of the assignment. On this basis, they will then be able to distinguish between [Higher Order Concerns (HOCs) and Later Order Concerns (LOCs)](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/ai-in-academic-writing/#c1268678). If the learning outcomes are unclear, the tutor will assist in defining them. This ensures that AI is used in a targeted manner to provide support without undermining the independence required in academic work.

retrieved.title: AI in your Studies: What is permitted?
retrieved.text:
AI can provide support at various points throughout your degree programme, such as idea structuring, understanding complex content and text revision. However, there are clear guidelines in place regarding the application and extent to which AI can be utilised in degree programmes.

The University of Vienna supports the responsible use of artificial intelligence in degree programmes, provided that such use is transparent, complies with regulations, upholds the [principles of academic integrity](https://backend.univie.ac.at/?id=225768), and that it supports you in achieving the intended learning outcomes.

Whether and how the use of AI is permitted always depends on the specific context. In case of doubt, the following applies: AI is not permitted unless clear permission has been granted.

[### Basic framework](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/ai-in-your-studies-what-is-permitted/basic-framework/)

[### What is permitted? And what is not?](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/ai-in-your-studies-what-is-permitted/what-is-permitted-and-what-is-not/)

[### Data protection and copyright](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/ai-in-your-studies-what-is-permitted/legal-requirements-data-protection-and-copyright/)

[## Things AI is not recommended for](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/ai-in-your-studies-what-is-permitted/things-ai-is-not-recommended-for/)

[### AI and examinations](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/ai-and-exams/)

retrieved.title: Maintaining independence: AI-generated or AI-supported?
retrieved.text: For a text produced with the assistance of AI to qualify as your own work, there must be a substantial human contribution that decisively shapes the form of the generated text. In this case, the AI tool merely serves in a supportive capacity as an executing instrument. This applies: - where a highly sophisticated prompt is used, which you have created yourself and whose formulation may require relevant knowledge; - where text is produced through the combination of strongly directive prompts that determine the essential structural decisions rather than leaving them to the AI tool; - where users further develop the generated text as a stimulus for reflection (cf. [Salden und Leschke 2023](https://hss-opus.ub.ruhr-uni-bochum.de/opus4/frontdoor/deliver/index/docId/9734/file/2023_03_06_Didaktik_Recht_KI_Hochschulbildung.pdf)) In these cases, AI complements the academic working process without replacing your independent contribution. What is crucial is that the substantive decisions remain yours. This is the case, for example, when you use AI: - to generate ideas or as a stimulus for reflection; - to obtain structural or outline suggestions that form the basis for further development (not for direct 1:1 adoption); - as a tool for reflection, for example to review arguments; - or to revise the language of your own texts. It is imperative that you critically examine, develop and embed AI-generated results within your own line of argument. The more actively you guide AI and the more decisions you take yourself, the clearer

retrieved.title: Checklist for the responsible use of AI
heading_path: Checklist for the responsible use of AI
retrieved.text: - Bear in mind that texts and documents you enter into AI may themselves be copyrighted: therefore, ensure that your input is not used for AI training. Honesty & Independence - Do not pass off AI-generated content as your own work - Differentiate between Higher Order Concerns (HOCs) and Later Order Concerns (LOCs) – depending on the learning outcomes. Transparency & traceability - Document which AI tools you have used, including the version. - Disclose the purpose and scope of the AI's use. - If AI-generated content has been copied verbatim, specify the prompt used. - Check whether detailed documentation of all prompts is necessary or practical. - If you used AI in a collaborative process, document how it contributed to idea development. - Only include AI tools in the bibliography if there is a publicly accessible link to the chat. Using AI as a reflection aid - Use AI as a Socratic dialogue partner that asks questions and provides food for thought. - Allow AI to assist you in developing research questions or argumentation structures, without outsourcing your intellectual performance. Clear distinction between AI support and your own contribution - Edit and review AI-generated content so that your own scientific voice remains recognisable. - Ensure that AI only supports the scientific writing process, but does not replace it.

retrieved.title: Maintaining independence: AI-generated or AI-supported?
heading_path: Controlling AI in a targeted manner and maintaining independence
retrieved.text:
The quality of AI-generated output depends to a large extent on how precisely and reflectively a request is formulated. AI tools do not independently respond to academic requirements; rather, they orient themselves to the specifications they receive through [prompts](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/controlling-ai-in-a-targeted-manner-the-importance-of-prompting-correctly/).

It is important to define the objective, context and framework conditions clearly, as this will ensure the most effective use of AI as a supportive tool. Through targeted prompting, the user retains control over the working process, determines the direction and depth of responses, and ensures that substantive decisions remain theirs. AI thus provides impulses – the academic contextualisation and further development remain the responsibility of the user.

Formulieren Sie Prompts so, dass die KI **nicht für Sie denkt oder schreibt**, sondern Sie beim eigenen Denken unterstützt. Bitten Sie um **Fragen, Alternativen, Rückmeldungen oder Strukturvorschläge**, nicht um fertige Texte oder inhaltliche Entscheidungen. Bewährt haben sich Prompts, die:

- die KI auf eine **unterstützende Rolle** festlegen (zB Feedback, Reflexionshilfe),
- klar benennen, **was die KI nicht tun soll** (zB keine fertigen Argumente),
- deutlich machen, dass **Auswahl, Bewertung und Weiterverarbeitung** bei Ihnen liegen.

**Dabei gilt:** Die Vorschläge dienen als Ausgangspunkt und müssen fachlich geprüft, eingeordnet und weiterentwickelt werden.

u:ai enables the creation of so-called tailored AIs. These are individually configured chatbots that permanently store your instructions.

- Such chatbots can optionally work with their own knowledge base (e.g., guidelines, templates), which is then always given priority. This significantly reduces [hallucinations](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/what-is-artificial-intelligence/#c1268300).
- Once you have built a chatbot, you can also share it with other people.

This means that recurring tasks can be handled more efficiently without having to explain what the AI should do each time.

In certain modules, an Internet search can be activated as an option. This accesses external information via the [Perplexity](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Perplexity.ai) service. This gives you access to up-to-date information.

When Internet search is activated, the entire chat history to date is used as context. This means that personal or confidential data used in this chat leaves the closed system of u:ai. Data protection compliance is then no longer fully guaranteed.

Therefore, never use personal or confidential content in the same chat as the Internet search, but start a new chat for the Internet search.

When using AI in higher education, it is not only functionality that plays a role, but above all the legal and data protection framework in which a tool is operated. U:ai was developed specifically for the university context and differs in key respects from freely accessible, commercial AI offerings.

reranked.title: u:ai - The AI tool of the University of Vienna
heading_path: Why should I use u:ai? Other AI tools can do so much more!
reranked.text: u:ai is operated in a secure cloud where all data is processed within the EU. The content you enter is not used for training AI models. This means that any text, documents, or questions you enter into u:ai remain in a protected environment. Bei vielen kommerziellen KI-Tools ist hingegen unklar, wo Daten gespeichert werden, wie lange sie aufbewahrt bleiben und ob sie für Trainingszwecke weiterverwendet werden. Daraus ergibt sich ein erhöhtes Risiko, insbesondere bei der Arbeit mit personenbezogenen, sensiblen oder studienrelevanten Inhalten What does this mean for me? - u:ai is compliant with data protection requirements insofar as your personal data and the information you enter are processed in accordance with the data protection standards of the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR). This does not apply if, within the same chat, you use the web search function or the Mistral model. - When processing the personal data of third parties, the principle of data minimisation applies as a general rule. This means that only the most essential personal data may be collected, processed and stored, in order to achive a specific defined objective. - In order to process the personal data of third parties or research data, you must obtain the consent of the persons concerned. In this context, please refer to the [information on handling research data](https://ctl.univie.ac.at/fileadmin/user_upload/z_ctl_neu/Angebote_fuer_Studierende/Handouts__Leitfaeden_und_Ressourcen/Handouts_und_Leitfaeden/2024_09_16_AI_and_research_data_EN.pdf). - **[Sensitive data](https://miro.com/app/board/uXjVGJDSrGw=/?moveToWidget=3458764658613096304&cot=14)** (this is a category of personal data that is

reranked.title: AI and Exams
heading_path: Important information for learning with AI
reranked.text: is increasing dependence on AI tools. If explanations, solution strategies or summaries are regularly delegated to AI, this may impair your ability to think through content independently and to solve problems on your own. Especially at the beginning of your studies, it is therefore important to use AI with restraint and to first develop your own learning strategies.

reranked.title: AI and Exams
heading_path: Examinations with AI: What does apply here?
reranked.text: permitted is prohibited. If you are unsure, you should always consult your lecturers in order to clarify expectations and framework conditions at an early stage. - Transparency and documentation: If you are permitted to use AI tools for written assignments, you must [fully disclose](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/citing-and-labelling-ai/#c1269189) such use in accordance with the principles of good academic practice. - Examination formats are changing: You should be prepared for greater emphasis on competences such as reflection and transfer. To ensure the independence of your performance, written assignments may be supplemented by oral components, plausibility checks or grade-relevant discussions. - Personal responsibility: It is imperative that you, the author, accept full responsibility for the accuracy, academic validity and integrity of the results submitted. As language models are not "knowledge machines" and may produce [hallucinations](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/what-is-artificial-intelligence/#c1268979), it is essential that all AI-generated content is critically reviewed. - Consequences of misconduct: The use of unauthorised AI tools constitutes academic misconduct. This results in a non-assessment (entry of an “X”) or the annulment of a grade that has already been awarded. In serious cases, the Rectorate may impose exclusion from studies for up to two semesters.

reranked.title: What is permitted? And what is not?
reranked.text:
Whether and how you may use AI in a course or in an examination is determined by the learning outcomes, the fundamental [principles of good academic practice](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/good-scientific-practice/#c1269035) and the requirements set by the respective lecturer. Therefore, always check out

- the information in u:find
- notices on Moodle
- information on specific assignments and examinations

In the event of ambiguity surrounding the use of AI, it is advisable to consult with the lecturer in advance to establish clear regulations. In instances where there is uncertainty, it is prudent to assume that AI is not permitted unless explicit permission has been obtained.

AI may be used, provided that responsibility for the content is retained and the independent nature of the work is preserved. For example, AI may be used to:

- generate ideas or structure topics
- gain an overview of a new topic or prepare for reading
- revise the language of one's own texts
- support reflection or check comprehensibility
- find examples or alternatives which are reviewed and contextualised independently

The use of AI must always be compatible with the requirements set by the lecturer and must not replace your independent contribution.

reranked.title: u:ai - The AI tool of the University of Vienna
reranked.text:
u:ai is the University of Vienna's AI platform, providing students with data protection-compliant access to various AI models. The platform was designed specifically for use in a university context and is part of the Austria-wide Academic AI project.

u:ai can support you in your learning, writing, and analysis—you always retain responsibility for the subject matter and control over the content.

#

u:ai is the University of Vienna's official access point to AI models such as ChatGPT in a secure cloud environment. This means:

- All data is processed within the EU (exception: Internet search, Mistral model)
- The content entered will not be used for training AI models.

However, U:ai was not trained with university-specific content. The AI therefore does not have internal knowledge about courses, exams, or internal institute regulations.

reranked.title: AI and Exams
heading_path: Important information for learning with AI
reranked.text: The use of AI tools can support the learning process, but it also presents challenges. In order to use AI sensibly and responsibly, it is important to consider a few fundamental aspects. - Due to its [functionality](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/what-is-artificial-intelligence/#c1268970), AI may produce incorrect, incomplete or imprecise information, even if it sounds plausible. Therefore, it is advisable to always compare responses with your own materials. - Subject-specific terminology may be used differently depending on the discipline. When asked to define technical terms, AI generally draws on the meaning most frequently represented in its training data. This may result in definitions that do not correspond to the specific discipline or that lack subject-specific nuances. Only adopt definitions or explanations if they align with the academic requirements of your course. - AI-supported learning tools can heavily structure the learning process, for example through predefined learning paths, automatic revision or recommended content. While this can be helpful, it also entails the risk that your learning process becomes overly externally directed: learning is individual and not every recommendation generated by AI suits your personal learning style. Therefore, consciously set your own priorities. - A common risk factor is increasing dependence on AI tools. If explanations, solution strategies or summaries are regularly delegated to AI, this may impair your ability to think through content independently and to solve problems on your own. Especially at the beginning of your

reranked.title: Academic work and writing with AI
reranked.text:
AI can provide valuable support in academic work and writing by generating ideas, structuring texts, revising language and style, organising literature, and clarifying questions of understanding. However, it is important to note that core academic tasks such as developing research questions, constructing arguments, interpreting, and evaluating must be carried out independently.

It is imperative that the use of AI is aligned with the learning outcomes, subjected to critical reflection, and made transparent. Responsibility for content, quality and academic integrity remains with you.

[#### Good scientific practice](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/good-scientific-practice/)

[#### Maintain independence](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/maintaining-independence-ai-supported-or-ai-generated/)

[#### Controlling AI by Prompting correctly](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/controlling-ai-in-a-targeted-manner-the-importance-of-prompting-correctly/)

[#### Citing and labelling AI](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/citing-and-labelling-ai/)

[#### AI in academic writing](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/ai-in-academic-writing/)

[#### Checklist for the responsible use of AI](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/checklist-for-the-responsible-use-of-ai/)

[#### AI and reading academic texts](https://ctl.univie.ac.at/fileadmin/user_upload/z_ctl_neu/Angebote_fuer_Studierende/Handouts__Leitfaeden_und_Ressourcen/Handouts_und_Leitfaeden/2024_09_16_AI_and_reading_academic_texts_EN.pdf)

[#### AI and research data](https://ctl.univie.ac.at/fileadmin/user_upload/z_ctl_neu/Angebote_fuer_Studierende/Handouts__Leitfaeden_und_Ressourcen/Handouts_und_Leitfaeden/2024_09_16_AI_and_research_data_EN.pdf)

reranked.title: Citing and labelling AI
reranked.text:
In order to ensure the visibility of your independent contribution, it is essential to present your work transparently and in a manner that allows for clear and accurate tracking of the use of AI. It is crucial to clearly distinguish the parts of the work that originate from you, ensuring that your independent performance is accurately assessed. Failure to disclose the use of AI could be considered a breach of the principles of good academic practice.

The responsibility for determining how the use of AI is to be made transparent lies with the respective lecturer, the study programme director (SPL) or the respective department. In the absence of specific requirements, established best practices may serve as a useful guideline.

A distinction is made between references within the text and entries in the bibliography and, secondly, between content generated entirely by AI and content produced with the assistance of AI.

reranked.title: Your AI tutor for academic writing
reranked.text:
AI-supported tools can be customised to align with specific learning outcomes and work requirements. It is quite simple to configure a personalised AI tutor chatbot that supports academic work without compromising independent performance.

- Such an AI tutor can be set up to assume tasks at the linguistic and formal level, for example revising texts, correcting style or organising literature.
- With regard to substantive tasks, such as developing the line of argument, the tutor's role is to provide support and act as a partner in reflection, in the sense of a Socratic dialogue. By posing targeted questions, the tutor can stimulate critical thinking and in-depth engagement with the content, without undermining users' independence or creative contribution.

Configured like this, the AI tutor can offer targeted support without replacing intellectual work.

Recommendation: your AI tutor should first request that you define the learning outcomes of the assignment. On this basis, they will then be able to distinguish between [Higher Order Concerns (HOCs) and Later Order Concerns (LOCs)](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/ai-in-academic-writing/#c1268678). If the learning outcomes are unclear, the tutor will assist in defining them. This ensures that AI is used in a targeted manner to provide support without undermining the independence required in academic work.

reranked.title: AI in your Studies: What is permitted?
reranked.text:
AI can provide support at various points throughout your degree programme, such as idea structuring, understanding complex content and text revision. However, there are clear guidelines in place regarding the application and extent to which AI can be utilised in degree programmes.

The University of Vienna supports the responsible use of artificial intelligence in degree programmes, provided that such use is transparent, complies with regulations, upholds the [principles of academic integrity](https://backend.univie.ac.at/?id=225768), and that it supports you in achieving the intended learning outcomes.

Whether and how the use of AI is permitted always depends on the specific context. In case of doubt, the following applies: AI is not permitted unless clear permission has been granted.

[### Basic framework](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/ai-in-your-studies-what-is-permitted/basic-framework/)

[### What is permitted? And what is not?](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/ai-in-your-studies-what-is-permitted/what-is-permitted-and-what-is-not/)

[### Data protection and copyright](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/ai-in-your-studies-what-is-permitted/legal-requirements-data-protection-and-copyright/)

[## Things AI is not recommended for](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/ai-in-your-studies-what-is-permitted/things-ai-is-not-recommended-for/)

[### AI and examinations](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/ai-and-exams/)

reranked.title: Maintaining independence: AI-generated or AI-supported?
reranked.text: For a text produced with the assistance of AI to qualify as your own work, there must be a substantial human contribution that decisively shapes the form of the generated text. In this case, the AI tool merely serves in a supportive capacity as an executing instrument. This applies: - where a highly sophisticated prompt is used, which you have created yourself and whose formulation may require relevant knowledge; - where text is produced through the combination of strongly directive prompts that determine the essential structural decisions rather than leaving them to the AI tool; - where users further develop the generated text as a stimulus for reflection (cf. [Salden und Leschke 2023](https://hss-opus.ub.ruhr-uni-bochum.de/opus4/frontdoor/deliver/index/docId/9734/file/2023_03_06_Didaktik_Recht_KI_Hochschulbildung.pdf)) In these cases, AI complements the academic working process without replacing your independent contribution. What is crucial is that the substantive decisions remain yours. This is the case, for example, when you use AI: - to generate ideas or as a stimulus for reflection; - to obtain structural or outline suggestions that form the basis for further development (not for direct 1:1 adoption); - as a tool for reflection, for example to review arguments; - or to revise the language of your own texts. It is imperative that you critically examine, develop and embed AI-generated results within your own line of argument. The more actively you guide AI and the more decisions you take yourself, the clearer

reranked.title: Checklist for the responsible use of AI
heading_path: Checklist for the responsible use of AI
reranked.text: - Bear in mind that texts and documents you enter into AI may themselves be copyrighted: therefore, ensure that your input is not used for AI training. Honesty & Independence - Do not pass off AI-generated content as your own work - Differentiate between Higher Order Concerns (HOCs) and Later Order Concerns (LOCs) – depending on the learning outcomes. Transparency & traceability - Document which AI tools you have used, including the version. - Disclose the purpose and scope of the AI's use. - If AI-generated content has been copied verbatim, specify the prompt used. - Check whether detailed documentation of all prompts is necessary or practical. - If you used AI in a collaborative process, document how it contributed to idea development. - Only include AI tools in the bibliography if there is a publicly accessible link to the chat. Using AI as a reflection aid - Use AI as a Socratic dialogue partner that asks questions and provides food for thought. - Allow AI to assist you in developing research questions or argumentation structures, without outsourcing your intellectual performance. Clear distinction between AI support and your own contribution - Edit and review AI-generated content so that your own scientific voice remains recognisable. - Ensure that AI only supports the scientific writing process, but does not replace it.

reranked.title: Maintaining independence: AI-generated or AI-supported?
heading_path: Controlling AI in a targeted manner and maintaining independence
reranked.text:
The quality of AI-generated output depends to a large extent on how precisely and reflectively a request is formulated. AI tools do not independently respond to academic requirements; rather, they orient themselves to the specifications they receive through [prompts](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/controlling-ai-in-a-targeted-manner-the-importance-of-prompting-correctly/).

It is important to define the objective, context and framework conditions clearly, as this will ensure the most effective use of AI as a supportive tool. Through targeted prompting, the user retains control over the working process, determines the direction and depth of responses, and ensures that substantive decisions remain theirs. AI thus provides impulses – the academic contextualisation and further development remain the responsibility of the user.

Formulieren Sie Prompts so, dass die KI **nicht für Sie denkt oder schreibt**, sondern Sie beim eigenen Denken unterstützt. Bitten Sie um **Fragen, Alternativen, Rückmeldungen oder Strukturvorschläge**, nicht um fertige Texte oder inhaltliche Entscheidungen. Bewährt haben sich Prompts, die:

- die KI auf eine **unterstützende Rolle** festlegen (zB Feedback, Reflexionshilfe),
- klar benennen, **was die KI nicht tun soll** (zB keine fertigen Argumente),
- deutlich machen, dass **Auswahl, Bewertung und Weiterverarbeitung** bei Ihnen liegen.

**Dabei gilt:** Die Vorschläge dienen als Ausgangspunkt und müssen fachlich geprüft, eingeordnet und weiterentwickelt werden.

selected.title: u:ai - The AI tool of the University of Vienna
selected.text:
To access u:ai, you need your u:account. Access is via [univie.academic-ai.at](https://univie.academic-ai.at)

You have a monthly credit of €20.00 available for queries. The cost of a specific query depends on the length of the question and the length of the answer (file uploads cost more than text queries, shorter texts less than long texts). You can view and monitor your credit balance directly in u:ai. Once the amount has been used up, you will not be able to use u:ai again until the following month.

Since the monthly credit is limited, u:ai should be used specifically for study and university-related questions. The credit is automatically recharged on the first day of each month. Unused credit cannot be carried over to the following month.

**Knowledge Cut-Off:**

U:ai is currently based on information that was available until October 2024. The system does not know anything that happened after that. To incorporate up-to-date information, you must activate the [Internet search](#c1268815).

selected.title: Basic framework for AI use
selected.text:
Although AI can provide support, it is important to remember that it should not be used to replace the work that is expected of you.

This recommendation can be put into practice by configuring an AI tool in a way that that the support it provides is aligned with the respective learning outcomes. For this purpose, an [AI tutor](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/ai-tutor-for-academic-writing/) can be developed that offers support while at the same time helping to safeguard independent work.

selected.title: Maintaining independence: AI-generated or AI-supported?
selected.text:
When you use AI in your studies, what is decisive is the extent of your own contribution. A helpful point of reference is the [distinction between AI-generated content and AI-supported work](https://www.europarl.europa.eu/doceo/document/TA-9-2020-0277_DE.html). This distinction can help you assess:

- what qualifies as your own work,
- what may be problematic,
- and how you should render the use of AI transparent.

The practice of directly sourcing passages or entire texts from an AI tool without substantial revision constitutes an infringement of academic integrity. In such instances, the fundamental decisions originate from the AI system, with the individual's contribution being confined to providing the prompt. This practice is in direct contravention of the established principles of academic integrity and may be problematic, particularly if it is not disclosed.

selected.title: Basic framework for AI use
selected.text:
The use of AI in degree programmes is not generally prohibited, but nor is it permitted without any restrictions. Whether and how you may use AI always depends on the specific context, such as:

- the course type
- the examination or assessment format
- the specific requirements set by the lecturer
- the respective learning objectives
- the [principles of good academic practice](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/good-scientific-practice/#c1269035)

As a general rule, you are responsible for all work that you submit, including any that has been created using AI tools. This implies that:

- You are responsible for the accuracy, quality and traceability of your work.
- Any incorrect statements, distortions or fabricated sources are your responsibility.
- While AI may support you in your work, the substantive contribution and independent thinking must be your own.

selected.title: Using AI in Your Studies
selected.text: Imagine you are tasked with writing an academic paper. You are aware of the importance of a clear research question, thorough research and a coherent line of argument. Yet instead of doing everything yourself, there is a new option: AI-supported tools can suggest literature, generate outlines, draft opening sentences and improve your writing style. Artificial intelligence has long become part of everyday student life. At the same time, its use in degree programmes raises important questions: What is permitted? Where are the boundaries? And what do students need to be aware of? On these pages, you will find concise and reliable information on the responsible use of AI in degree programmes at the University of Vienna. You will learn under which conditions AI may be used, which specific provisions apply to examinations and what requires particular attention when using AI. The content is based on the current regulations of the University of Vienna and is intended for students of all disciplines. The aim is to support a responsible, transparent and reflective use of AI. [### What Is Artificial Intelligence?](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/what-is-artificial-intelligence/) [### AI in Your Studies: What Is Permitted?](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/ai-in-your-studies-what-is-permitted/) [### Academic work and writing](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/) [### AI and examinations](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/ai-and-exams/) [## u:ai – The University of Vienna's AI tool](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/uai-the-university-of-viennas-ai-tool/) [### Further information](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/further-information/) Generative AI (OpenAI, ChatGPT 5.2) was used on this website and its subpages to adapt content for the target audience. It was

selected.title: Basic framework for AI use
selected.text: tools may be used therefore depends to a significant extent on the respective learning outcome, which may vary from course to course. Example: - When translating a passage from French in an academic paper on a specific topic, it is important to engage with the concrete content. In this case, the use of an AI translation tool may be unproblematic, provided that the translation is checked for accuracy and the use of AI is disclosed. - In a language degree programme, translation itself is a central learning outcome. Therefore, if a translation task is delegated to an AI tool in this context, the independent performance expected of students would be undermined. If AI performs the work on your behalf, this contravenes the principles of academic integrity. Whether you are permitted to use artificial intelligence in your degree programme therefore primarily depends on whether the required learning outcomes and your independent contribution are preserved.

- and how you should render the use of AI transparent.

As a general rule, you are responsible for all work that you submit, including any that has been created using AI tools. This implies that:

- You are responsible for the accuracy, quality and traceability of your work.
- Any incorrect statements, distortions or fabricated sources are your responsibility.
- While AI may support you in your work, the substantive contribution and independent thinking must be your own.

And what do students need to be aware of? On these pages, you will find concise and reliable information on the responsible use of AI in degree programmes at the University of Vienna. You will learn under which conditions AI may be used, which specific provisions apply to examinations and what requires particular attention when using AI. The content is based on the current regulations of the University of Vienna and is intended for students of all disciplines. The aim is to support a responsible, transparent and reflective use of AI. In this case, the use of an AI translation tool may be unproblematic, provided that the translation is checked for accuracy and the use of AI is disclosed. - In a language degree programme, translation itself is a central learning outcome. 1.title: u:ai - The AI tool of the University of Vienna

prompt_messages.content:
You are a concise assistant for prospective and current University of Vienna students. Answer the question using ONLY the numbered sources provided.
Rules:
- Base every statement on the sources; never add outside knowledge or guess.
- Cite each claim inline with the bracket number(s) of the source(s) you used, e.g. [1] or [2][3]. Cite only sources you actually relied on.
- If the sources do not contain the answer, reply that you don't know, and do NOT cite any sources.
- Keep the answer short and direct (it may be read aloud by a voice assistant).

Sources:
[1] u:ai - The AI tool of the University of Vienna — Access and general conditions
To access u:ai, you need your u:account. Access is via [univie.academic-ai.at](https://univie.academic-ai.at)

You have a monthly credit of €20.00 available for queries. The cost of a specific query depends on the length of the question and the length of the answer (file uploads cost more than text queries, shorter texts less than long texts). You can view and monitor your credit balance directly in u:ai. Once the amount has been used up, you will not be able to use u:ai again until the following month.

Since the monthly credit is limited, u:ai should be used specifically for study and university-related questions. The credit is automatically recharged on the first day of each month. Unused credit cannot be carried over to the following month.

**Knowledge Cut-Off:**

U:ai is currently based on information that was available until October 2024. The system does not know anything that happened after that. To incorporate up-to-date information, you must activate the [Internet search](#c1268815).

[2] Basic framework for AI use — Learning outcomes and independence > In short:
Although AI can provide support, it is important to remember that it should not be used to replace the work that is expected of you.

This recommendation can be put into practice by configuring an AI tool in a way that that the support it provides is aligned with the respective learning outcomes. For this purpose, an [AI tutor](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/ai-tutor-for-academic-writing/) can be developed that offers support while at the same time helping to safeguard independent work.

[3] Maintaining independence: AI-generated or AI-supported?
When you use AI in your studies, what is decisive is the extent of your own contribution. A helpful point of reference is the [distinction between AI-generated content and AI-supported work](https://www.europarl.europa.eu/doceo/document/TA-9-2020-0277_DE.html). This distinction can help you assess:

- what qualifies as your own work,
- what may be problematic,
- and how you should render the use of AI transparent.

The practice of directly sourcing passages or entire texts from an AI tool without substantial revision constitutes an infringement of academic integrity. In such instances, the fundamental decisions originate from the AI system, with the individual's contribution being confined to providing the prompt. This practice is in direct contravention of the established principles of academic integrity and may be problematic, particularly if it is not disclosed.

[4] Basic framework for AI use
The use of AI in degree programmes is not generally prohibited, but nor is it permitted without any restrictions. Whether and how you may use AI always depends on the specific context, such as:

- the course type
- the examination or assessment format
- the specific requirements set by the lecturer
- the respective learning objectives
- the [principles of good academic practice](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/good-scientific-practice/#c1269035)

As a general rule, you are responsible for all work that you submit, including any that has been created using AI tools. This implies that:

- You are responsible for the accuracy, quality and traceability of your work.
- Any incorrect statements, distortions or fabricated sources are your responsibility.
- While AI may support you in your work, the substantive contribution and independent thinking must be your own.

[5] Using AI in Your Studies
Imagine you are tasked with writing an academic paper. You are aware of the importance of a clear research question, thorough research and a coherent line of argument. Yet instead of doing everything yourself, there is a new option: AI-supported tools can suggest literature, generate outlines, draft opening sentences and improve your writing style. Artificial intelligence has long become part of everyday student life. At the same time, its use in degree programmes raises important questions: What is permitted? Where are the boundaries? And what do students need to be aware of? On these pages, you will find concise and reliable information on the responsible use of AI in degree programmes at the University of Vienna. You will learn under which conditions AI may be used, which specific provisions apply to examinations and what requires particular attention when using AI. The content is based on the current regulations of the University of Vienna and is intended for students of all disciplines. The aim is to support a responsible, transparent and reflective use of AI. [### What Is Artificial Intelligence?](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/what-is-artificial-intelligence/) [### AI in Your Studies: What Is Permitted?](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/ai-in-your-studies-what-is-permitted/) [### Academic work and writing](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/) [### AI and examinations](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/ai-and-exams/) [## u:ai – The University of Vienna's AI tool](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/uai-the-university-of-viennas-ai-tool/) [### Further information](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/further-information/) Generative AI (OpenAI, ChatGPT 5.2) was used on this website and its subpages to adapt content for the target audience. It was

[6] Basic framework for AI use — Learning outcomes and independence
tools may be used therefore depends to a significant extent on the respective learning outcome, which may vary from course to course. Example: - When translating a passage from French in an academic paper on a specific topic, it is important to engage with the concrete content. In this case, the use of an AI translation tool may be unproblematic, provided that the translation is checked for accuracy and the use of AI is disclosed. - In a language degree programme, translation itself is a central learning outcome. Therefore, if a translation task is delegated to an AI tool in this context, the independent performance expected of students would be undermined. If AI performs the work on your behalf, this contravenes the principles of academic integrity. Whether you are permitted to use artificial intelligence in your degree programme therefore primarily depends on whether the required learning outcomes and your independent contribution are preserved.

Question: that uni ai thing — how much free usage do i get a month and does it stack up if i don't use it all

generate.raw_response: You have a monthly credit of €20.00 available for queries on u:ai [1]. If you don't use it all, the unused credit cannot be carried over to the following month [1].

answer: You have a monthly credit of €20.00 available for queries on u:ai [1]. If you don't use it all, the unused credit cannot be carried over to the following month [1].
